Let's actually move on to wireframing a website. Now, open up a Word document if you're taking notes on your computer, or start a new piece of paper. And when I say wireframe a website, what I really mean is that we're going to write all of the text for a website. We're going to write about five sections of a landing page, maybe four, four sections of a landing page together, and we're going to do that right now. Before we do that though, here's what I think your website should do. Your website needs to pass the grunt test. And what do I mean by grunt test? I mean, if you gave your laptop to a caveman sitting in a cave, and you gave that caveman five seconds to look at your website, five seconds, very primitive thinker, can the caveman grunt the answer to three questions? What do you offer? How will it make my life better? And what do I need to do to buy it? Why don't you take a picture of that? Take a picture or a screenshot. I think your website, the header on your website, absolutely needs to answer these three questions. If you, your customer cannot answer these three questions in five seconds, you're losing money. Okay, look at this website. This is a sample website my designer made. When you look at it, it's really beautiful, isn't it? And you read the text, your journey starts here, that sounds so poetic. Look closely at the website. What do they offer? I don't know. Do you know? How will it make your life better? I don't know. What do you need to do to buy it? So somebody paid $10,000 for this stupid website. And they looked at it and thought it's so beautiful. And the language is so poetic. Your journey starts here. I love that. Meanwhile, they're not selling anybody, anything to anybody. This is a bad website. So let's look at a good website. The header for a good website is going to answer these three questions. So let's go to Circuit E-Bikes, our sample company. The I moves in a Z pattern across the page. It starts at the top left, goes to the top right, swings across the middle, and goes across the bottom. So I'm going to stand and I'm going to show you what the I does. Now, the I also makes an F pattern. It actually does two things. But the I starts here, goes here, goes across the bottom, across the middle, and across the bottom. It makes a Z. Here, here, here, and here. So what we want to do is we want to put the words from your brand script in the places where the fish swim. The fish swim in this pattern, and we got to put our hooks in that pattern. If you've got a big paragraph here, people see that paragraph and think, I've got to burn a lot of calories to read this website. I'm out. So let's look at Circuit E-Bikes. We know that it's an E-Bike. What do you offer? We passed the grunt test, at least the first part of it. What do I need to do to buy it? I need to find a dealer. How will it make my life better? It'll make me the leader of the pack. I get a revolutionary new bike. I save the environment, save money, and save time. So one, two, three, four, five things I get when I buy your E-Bike. I call that value stacking. So let's look at what effectively this header does. First, it passes the grunt test in five seconds. What are they selling? An E-Bike. How's it going to make my life better? I know it's going to save the environment, and it's going to make me some kind of leader of the pack. Great. What do I need to buy? Find a dealer. Find a dealer is repeated twice. Notice there's not a whole bunch of links up here. Not a whole bunch of other text on the website. It's very clean. So let's look at what the website is doing in terms of value stacking. This E-Bike is $3,000. So if it's $3,000 for a $3,000 investment, I get a $3,000 E-Bike. However, it will make me the leader of the pack. Gosh, that seems like emotionally that's worth about $1,000 more bucks. So now I get a $4,000 E-Bike for $3,000. And it's revolutionary. That's a $5,000 E-Bike for $3,000. Plus, I'll save the environment. You know, I'll pay money to save the environment. This is a $6,000 value for $3,000. It's going to save me money. Gosh, I'd pay money to save money, wouldn't you? So that's $7,000 return for a $3,000 E-Bike. It's going to save me time. Time is very valuable. That's an $8,000 product that I'm buying for $3,000. It's a $5,000 deal I'm getting. I just added $5,000 in value to an E-Bike with words that were free. Does that make sense? Jazz hands, if that makes sense. Great, great, great. So, oh, I love it. So, that's how we want to create the header of your website. So what I want you to do there, and the header, by the way, is the most important part. I think it's 50% of the effectiveness of your messaging is the header of your website. Some of you just need to create a new header for your website and let the rest of it drop down and just keep it as it is, but get that new header there. It frames the whole rest of the conversation. Okay, so on page 9, I want you to basically take the template that I gave you, and we'll put it back up, and I want you to refill out on page 9 a template if you had to use these words to create a header for your company. Don't create a new header for an E-Bike. Create a header for your company, but use the boxes that I'm giving you here. Okay, you've got your tagline, maybe, your direct call to action, your main text in the middle, your main call to action again, and the value stack across the bottom. The lines on this worksheet are not actually lining up the same as they do in the deal, so that's a mistake on my part. But go ahead and fill out what's up here, put it down here. Make sense? Give me one hand, one jazz hand if that makes sense. Looking good. All right, you've got five minutes to create a header for your website. Yep. Can we see that grunt test slide? Just one more time, there's a couple people that wanted to grab that screenshot. Yep, you ready? As we pass this out. Here it is, guys. Go ahead and grab it. Okay, look. Did it come through? Oh, there we go. I see all three up there. Yep, grab a screenshot of that. Thank you. And then I'll put it back on the header. There you go. Now, you create your version of that. You got five minutes. All right, we've had some questions about whether we're talking about a home page or a sales landing page. And really, we're talking about a landing page, do the exact same principles apply. I would say the only real difference is, you know, on a home page, you're going to have a lot more information. So you're going to have more links at the top navigation. But, you know, if you go to my website, the main website has a lot of links, but when you click on anything on the website, it goes to a landing page where all those links are left out. So, you know, same principles, but on a home page, I'll give you more grace in terms of including more. All right, what you just created was the header of the website. And it's, like I said, I think it's up to 50% of your website copy and effectiveness is just whether you get that copy right. Now, we started with the positive. Your life can be good if you buy our product. What we want to do next is say, but it's not. We want to get to the problem as fast as possible. And so the number two section of your landing page is going to be the stakes. You're going to remind customers what terrible thing could happen if they don't buy your product or service. That's part two. And it's really where you set the hook. This website, as you scroll down, is telling a story. You want this kind of life, but you don't have it. What you've got is traffic. And so notice the internal, external, and philosophical are all here. Life is too short to sit in traffic. That's philosophical. Traffic is no fun. That's internal. Don't be held back. That's internal. You were designed for more. That's philosophical. And then the external, of course, is the traffic, which we represent in visual. So what you want to do is go ahead and write the negative. Now, you can do this many different ways. You can do like I did with a few sentences. You can actually create bullet points. But I do want you to have a headline above this section that just says, you don't have to struggle with this anymore. Or something like a lot of customers are struggling with these problems. And then you've got six or nine bullet points. You can get creative here. Remember, you're writing a song, but you want that negative stuff from your brand script. This is where it goes, because this is what you are saving customers from. Okay? So right there on page 10, you're going to fill out the second section of your landing page. And it's the stakes. What's at stake if you do not buy my product? Here's what happens to you. Okay, you've got five minutes. All right. Well, you've got two sections of your website done. This excites me from my perspective, because you have a one-liner and two sections of your website that are coming from a clear message. And you don't know this yet, but you're making more and more money as the afternoon goes along. All you've got to do is get these things into the eyeballs of your customers. That's it. And once they read it and hear it, you're going to do a lot better. All right. I'm not going to give you as much time. I'm only going to give you three minutes, because from your brand script, you now want to put the next section of your website, which is your value proposition. So you're going to take your, from your success bucket of your website, and we're going to do the opposite of what we just did in stakes. We're going to tell them what great things can happen if they buy your product or service. So if you buy an e-bike, you save time, you take back control of your life, you avoid parking hassles, you save money on gas, and you arrive on time, if not earlier. You get all that with this e-bike. So what do I get when I buy your product? Okay. So feel free to use bullet points where you list a bunch of stuff. Feel free to use one short paragraph where you list what they get. Notice how visually this is broken up so that it looks like it's easy to read. Big bold headlines with small paragraphs underneath. Notice also that I keep putting the direct call to action on every section. Why do I do that? Because it's the cash register. You do not want to hide the cash register, install number two of the bathroom. You want it right out there in front where everybody can find it. Anytime somebody wants to hand you money in exchange for your product and service, you should have a button there allowing them to do that. Okay, you're actually, I think you're going to get five minutes on this one as well, but go ahead and fill out the value proposition on page 11 of your workbook. Go ahead. Okay. We got yet another hook in the water. We have now what, three, four sections of this website. Landing page, forgive me, absolutely fantastic. Okay. So now that you have done your value proposition, it's time to introduce yourself as a guide. And so the next section of your website is page 12. And what I want you to do, not going to give you a whole lot of time on this one, but I want you to go ahead and give me your empathetic statement as well as a demonstration of competency. So on our website it says we don't just care about bikes, we care about you. Then we demonstrate competency by saying more than 20,000 happy riders, more than five million gallons of gas saved, more than 100,000 commute hours saved. We care about you and we can solve your problem. That's the one, two punch of the guide. All right? So in this section, you're going to take that kind of language as it relates to your brand and put it down there at the bottom of page 12. If I'm not mistaken, you have three minutes, just three minutes because it shouldn't take you that long. All right? Hopefully you were able to do that pretty easily. Positioning yourself as a guide is really paramount and that's going to take you really, really far in terms of building that relationship with your customer. All right? So we keep moving through the workshop and your landing page. We get to page 13 and page 13 is really just a chance for you to put all those other price choices and divisions of your company. You're kind of going down, we sell more than one product. Where do we get to our actual products? You put that on your landing page here and of course if you click on any of these, you're just going to repeat the formula. So if I click on the mountain climber or the commuter or the beachcomber, I'm going to have the exact same formula that was used to create the other website. So we're not going to take any time to do this. If you need that section, that's where you do it. Hang on to your workbook and fill that out. And then the plan. And the plan is those three steps. Here's how we work together. You do this, then you do this, then I do this. So with our chef, it was I come over to your house and do an intake and then I cook a one meal for you at cost and then we decide whether or not we want to move forward with the schedule. Those three phases really make it much easier to work with you. So if you have a chance, you want to say, look, get fitted, choose a bike and lead the pack. What are the three steps, baby steps for doing work with you? And so that is going to be on page 14 of your workbook. And again, I'm just going to give you a few minutes to fill out page 14. Okay. What is the plan moving your customer forward? Okay. Remember with each section of this landing page, your sales pitch is just getting stronger and stronger. And you don't need all of them, but the more of this language you can include in that landing page, the better the response is going to be from your customers. The next is on page 15, it's that explanatory paragraph. This is where you would put two things. The first is your one liner and then you want something that says, read more. So it's your one liner. And then if you click continue reading or read more, your brand script script pops down below it. So this is where you put your one liner and your brand script script, both of which you've already written. And so this is where you put that. And if you want to, you can go ahead and fill that out in your workbook later. The reason is you can actually just hand this workbook to a graphic artist and tell them to create a landing page for you. And it's going to save a lot of money and it's going to save them a lot of time. But that's where that goes. We're not going to give you any time to do that because you already wrote that down earlier. Next is an ad for your lead generator. So if you're not collecting email addresses, I can't imagine that you're not, but if you're not, you want to go ahead and start collecting email addresses with some sort of lead generator. And so this is going to be, we'll talk about this in the next section after the break. But that's where you put the ad for the lead generator. Then finally on page 17, it's the bottom of the landing page. This is where you put the junk drawer. And the junk drawer is everything that you thought was important before this workshop is what you're going to put in the junk drawer. People will look for contact information. They'll look for login. They'll look for employment opportunities. If they're going to look for it, put it down in the junk drawer because we don't want anything deluding people's attention from the cash register, the direct call to action. So those are five sections or six sections of a landing page and you want all of those on your landing page. You can have more sections than that. You can have less. But I really like these as elements that invite people into a story. Okay, in terms of our overall sales funnel then, we've got our one liner, our wire framed website we just did. When we get back from the break, we're going to talk really quickly about some lead generator ideas that have worked well for my company. And we're going to talk about what needs to be in some of these follow up emails. All right? So we will take a break. We're also going to do some Q&A after the break. All right, so go ahead. We're going to do a 15 minute break. We're going to do a 15 minute break. I will see you back here a little bit after three. We'll give you a little countdown clock in the last few minutes to let you know we're coming back.
